Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## APPROXIMATING INSTANCE-DEPENDENT NOISE VIA INSTANCE-CONFIDENCE EMBEDDING

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Label noise in multiclass classification is a major obstacle to the deployment of learning systems. However, unlike the widely used _class-conditional noise_ (CCN) assumption that the noisy label is independent of the input feature given the true label, label noise in real-world datasets can be aleatory and heavily dependent on individual instances. In this work, we investigate the _instance-dependent noise_ (IDN) model and propose an efficient approximation of IDN to capture the instancespecific label corruption. Concretely, noting the fact that most columns of the IDN transition matrix have only limited influence on the class-posterior estimation, we propose a variational approximation that uses a single-scalar _confidence_ parameter. To cope with the situation where the mapping from the instance to its confidence value could vary significantly for two adjacent instances, we suggest using _instance embedding_ that assigns a trainable parameter to each instance. The resulting _instance-confidence embedding_ (ICE) method not only performs well under label noise but also can effectively detect ambiguous or mislabeled instances. We validate its utility on various image and text classification tasks.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

In modern machine learning, large-scale data has become indispensable (Russakovsky et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2019a). A prevalent approach to collecting large-scale labeled datasets is to use imperfect sources such as crowdsourcing and web crawling (Fergus et al., 2005; Schroff et al., 2010; Wang et al., 2019a), which is usually less expensive and time-consuming than manual annotation by domain experts. However, such methods inevitably introduce label noise that may lead to overfitting and hurt the generalization of deep models (Arpit et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2017).

In such situations, it is often beneficial to (i) remove mislabeled data or abstain from using confusing instances (Hara et al., 2019; Thulasidasan et al., 2019); (ii) increase robustness and reduce harmful influences of noisy labels (Malach & Shalev-Shwartz, 2017; Mirzasoleiman et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2020); or (iii) explicitly model the transition from the unobservable true label to the noisy observation (Goldberger & Ben-Reuven, 2017; Patrini et al., 2017; Xia et al., 2020). In this work, we focus on explicit modeling of the label corruption process, which is model-agnostic and data-efficient.

Most existing studies in this direction employ the _class-conditional noise_ (CCN) assumption, i.e., the noisy label is independent of the input feature given the true label (Angluin & Laird, 1988; Natarajan et al., 2013; Patrini et al., 2017). However, this assumption could be too strong to fit some real-world data well (Xiao et al., 2015; Chen et al., 2021; Liu, 2021). More importantly, CCN only captures the general label flipping patterns between classes for all instances. In applications such as data cleansing and human-in-the-loop interaction, instance-specific noise information itself could be of central interest. This urges us to consider not only the class-conditional noise pattern but also the instance-specific noise modeling.

To handle this problem, in this work, we study the _instance-dependent noise_ (IDN) model, where the noisy label also depends on the input. Several methods have been reported in the literature, but they either only focus on binary classification under strong assumptions (Menon et al., 2018; Cheng et al., 2020), are based on domain-specific knowledge (Xia et al., 2020), or need extra supervision (Berthon et al., 2021). In contrast, we propose a simple domain-agnostic approximation method for the multiclass IDN model, referred to as _instance-confidence embedding_ (ICE). Concretely, to avoid estimating the noise transition matrix for each instance, we propose a variational approximation that

Then, we suggest using _instance embedding_ that assigns a trainable parameter to each instance because the mapping from the instance to its confidence value could be non-smooth and is usually not required to generalize to unseen examples (Section 3.3). Lastly, we show the effectiveness of the proposed method and its ability to detect ambiguous or mislabeled instances through experiments on various image and text classification tasks (Section 5).

## 2 PROBLEM: INSTANCE-DEPENDENT NOISE

In this section, we give a brief overview of learning with _instance-dependent noise_ (IDN).

## 2.1 NOTATION

Consider a _K_ -class classification problem, where _X ∈X_ is the _input feature_ and _Y ∈{_ 1 _, . . . , K}_ is the unobservable categorical _true label_ . We assume that the _clean class-posterior p_ ( _Y |X_ ) comes from a parametric family of distributions:

where _**p** ∈_ ∆ _[K][−]_[1] is the probability parameter for _Y_ in the ( _K −_ 1)-dimensional probability simplex ∆ _[K][−]_[1] , and _f_ : _X →_ ∆ _[K][−]_[1] is a differentiable function parameterized by _φ_ that maps the feature _X_ to its corresponding probability parameter _**p**_ . Then, let _Y_[�] _∈{_ 1 _, . . . , K}_ be the _noisy label_ . The goal is to predict _Y_ from _X_ based on a finite i.i.d. sample of ( _X, Y_[�] )-pairs.

## 2.2 DEPENDENCE

Next, we introduce the dependence structure between _X_ , _Y_ , and _Y_[�] , which characterize different noise models. The graphical representations of noise models are illustrated in Fig. 1.
In IDN, we assume that the joint distribution of _X_ , _Y_ , and _Y_[�] can be factorized as follows:

That is, the noisy label _Y_[�] depends on both the instance _X_ and the true label _Y_ . Then, the _noisy class-posterior p_ ( _Y_[�] _|X_ ) can be obtained by marginalizing _p_ ( _Y, Y_[�] _|X_ ) over _Y_ :

Note that _p_ ( _Y_[�] _|Y, X_ ) plays a central role in IDN. Since both _Y_ and _Y_[�] are categorical random variables, for a certain instance _x_ , _p_ ( _Y_[�] _|Y, X_ = _x_ ) can be seen as a _K × K_ stochastic matrix _**T**_ ( _x_ ), whose elements are _**T** ij_ ( _x_ ) := _p_ ( _Y_[�] = _j|Y_ = _i, X_ = _x_ ) for _i, j ∈{_ 1 _, . . . , K}_ . Conventionally, _**T**_ ( _x_ ) is called a _noise transition matrix_ (Patrini et al., 2017). Then, _p_ ( _Y_[�] _|Y, X_ ) can be regarded as a matrix-valued function _**T**_ : _X →_ [0 _,_ 1] _[K][×][K]_ that maps each instance _x_ to its corresponding IDN transition matrix _**T**_ ( _x_ ). Without any restriction, we need _K × K_ parameters for each instance _x_ .

## 2.3 APPROACH

Owing to its complexity, IDN has only been studied to a limited extent but is of great interest recently. A straightforward method is to jointly estimate the matrix-valued function _**T**_ ( _x_ ) as well as the clean class-posterior _pφ_ ( _Y |X_ ) using neural networks (Goldberger & Ben-Reuven, 2017). However, the estimation error of _**T**_ ( _x_ ) could be high, which deteriorates the classification performance. Another direction is to restrict the problem under certain conditions, so that we can provide theoretical guarantees (Menon et al., 2018; Cheng et al., 2020). However, existing work mainly focused on binary classification.

A promising approach is to approximate IDN using a simpler dependence structure, such as a mixture of noises with different semantic meanings (Xiao et al., 2015) or a weighted combination of noises that depend on parts of the instance (Xia et al., 2020). In this work, we also suggest that it might be unnecessary to obtain a _K × K_ matrix for each instance _x_ : Note that _pφ_ ( _Y_[�] _|x_ ) can be seen as a linear combination of columns of _**T**_ ( _x_ ) weighted by _pφ_ ( _Y |x_ ); If the maximum value of _pφ_ ( _Y |x_ ) is close to 1, i.e., the label of the instance _x_ is almost deterministic, the estimation of _K −_ 1 columns of _**T**_ ( _x_ ) has only limited influence on the estimated noisy class-posterior _p_ �( _Y_[�] _|x_ ). This suggests the possibility of using a relatively simple model to approximate _p_ ( _Y_[�] _|X_ ) in real-world applications. In this work, we consider a _single-parameter_ approximation for each instance, which is introduced in Section 3.2 and illustrated in Fig. 2.

Another issue is that existing methods still introduce some level of smoothness w.r.t. _x_ into _**T**_ ( _x_ ) (Goldberger & Ben-Reuven, 2017; Xiao et al., 2015; Xia et al., 2020). In real-world problems, however, we can only access a finite sample of ( _X, Y_[�] )-pairs that are possibly annotated by nonexperts or web crawlers (Fergus et al., 2005). Thus, the label noise could be aleatory and _**T**_ ( _x_ ) could vary significantly for two adjacent instances. Also, the classifier _pφ_ ( _Y |X_ ) is desired but the generalization of _**T**_ ( _x_ ) to unseen examples is usually dispensable. This inspires us to use _instance embedding_ instead of neural network approximation, which is discussed in Section 3.3 and demonstrated in Fig. 3.

## 3 PROPOSED METHOD

In this section, we present our proposed method, _instance-confidence embedding_ (ICE).

## 3.1 VARIATIONAL LOWER BOUND

Note that _**T**_ ( _x_ ) serves as a _linear mapping_ from _**p**_ to _**q**_ (Eq. (3)). Due to the difficulty of estimating the matrix-valued function _**T**_ ( _x_ ), we use a simpler function _qθ,φ_ ( _Y_[�] _|X_ ) parameterized by _θ_ as a _variational approximation_ to _pφ_ ( _Y_[�] _|X_ ). The choice of the approximation family is discussed in Section 3.2.
Then, let us consider the _expected log-likelihood_ as the learning objective, which can be rewritten as

where _D_ KL denotes the Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence, and the second term is

Since the KL-divergence is always non-negative, this term gives a _variational lower bound_ of the expected log-likelihood. Then, we have the following learning objective to maximize:
In practice, the expectation can be approximated using the empirical distribution based on a finite i.i.d. sample of ( _X, Y_[�] )-pairs.

This inspires us to go a step further and use **single-parameter approximations** (▲ _�→_ ▽) _qθ,φ_ ( _Y_[�] _|x_ ) (Eqs. (8) and (9)).

## 3.2 VARIATIONAL APPROXIMATION

Next, we discuss the choice of the variational approximation family of _qθ,φ_ ( _Y_[�] _|X_ ).

To approximate the effect of multiplying an IDN transition matrix _**T**_ ( _x_ ) that requires _K × K_ parameters for each instance _x_ , in this work, we use a simpler transformation from _**p**_ to _**q**_ , which is not necessarily linear. Compared with estimating a full matrix for each instance without any restriction (Goldberger & Ben-Reuven, 2017), obtaining only an approximation may cause higher approximation error, but on the other hand, it may reduce estimation error and thus improve the classification performance. The high estimation error of complex models might be more harmful, which is empirically validated in Section 5. It is also the case when using CCN as an approximation of IDN to balance this trade-off. The difference is that CCN obtains a _complete_ transition matrix common to _all_ instances, but ICE aims to obtain an _approximated_ trend for _each_ instance, which gives useful instance-specific noise information.

Then, we suggest to use a _single-scalar parameter C ∈_ [0 _,_ 1] for each instance to control this approximation, which is useful for sorting and comparing training examples. This design might also be useful in data cleansing, learning with rejection, or active learning (Hara et al., 2019; Thulasidasan et al., 2019; Charoenphakdee et al., 2021). This parameter is referred to as the _confidence_ and is obtained via a function _g_ : _X →_ [0 _,_ 1] parameterized by _θ_ , i.e., _C_ = _g_ ( _X_ ; _θ_ ). Then, _θ_ can be regarded as the collection of _C_ for all instances. The confidence _C_ plays a central role in our method, where _C_ = 0 means that the instance is ambiguous or mislabeled and thus the classifier should not give a confident prediction.

Finally, we need to design a transformation from _**p**_ to _**q**_ parameterized by the confidence _C_ . We denote this function by _h_ : ∆ _[K][−]_[1] _→_ ∆ _[K][−]_[1] . In summary, _qφ,θ_ ( _Y_[�] _|X_ ) takes the following form:

Next, we analyze what characteristics _h_ needs to have. First, we suggest that _h_ needs not necessarily to be a linear transformation because the transformation is instance-dependent and any function that maps _**p**_ to _**q**_ as close as possible for a certain instance _x_ would suffice. Second, we require that arg max( _**p**_ ) = arg max( _**q**_ ), i.e., _**q**_ = _h_ ( _**p**_ ; _C_ ) should be an _argmax-preserving_ function so that the top-1 index of the probability vector does not change. This is because _h_ should only affect the confidence of the prediction, not the final decision. Otherwise, if _h_ is too flexible and is able to map a confident prediction to a different confident prediction, then the output of _f_ could be arbitrary, and consequently, no information of the true label can be learned from the noisy label supervision.

Based on this motivation and the aforementioned semantics of _C_ , we require that _h_ ( _**p**_ ; 1) = _**p**_ and _h_ ( _**p**_ ; 0) = _**u**_ , where _**u** ∈_ ∆ _[K][−]_[1] is the uniform probability vector ( _**u** i_ = _K_ 1[).][Then, when the] confidence _C_ is high, the classifier gives a prediction closer to the original _confident prediction_ _**p**_ ; and when the confidence _C_ is low, the classifier tends to give a _random guess_ _**u**_ .

## 3.3 INSTANCE EMBEDDING

The last piece of our method is the choice of _g_ : _X →_ [0 _,_ 1], the function that maps the instance _x_ to its confidence value _C_ . It is also possible to use a neural network to approximate this function. However, because we usually only have a limited number of training examples and _g_ could be non-smooth w.r.t. its input _x_ , _g_ may not be well approximated by a neural network with similar complexity to the classifier _f_ , which is illustrated in an example in Fig. 3. Further, _g_ may be rarely needed after training so its generalization ability is not required in many cases.

Based on these facts, we propose to use _instance embedding_ , i.e., to assign a trainable parameter to each instance _x_ . In other words, the only feature for an instance we use is its _index_ in the training dataset. In this way, _g_ is expressive and flexible but cannot be used for predicting the confidence of unseen instances. Accordingly, for a training dataset of size _N_ , we need _N_ parameters for a one-dimensional instance embedding.

This seems to be a high additional computational cost when the dataset size is large, but it is often acceptable, because (i) in modern deep learning, it is common to use over-parameterized models (Nakkiran et al., 2019), and the number of instances is usually not as large as the number of parameters of the classifier _f_ (e.g., CIFAR-10 (Krizhevsky, 2009): 5 _×_ 10[4] , ResNet-18 (He et al., 2016): _∼_ 1 _×_ 10[7] ); and (ii) the gradient of the instance embedding is sparse and only a small subset of parameters needs to be updated at each iteration.

The idea of associating an entity with a scalar or vector embedding using a simple lookup table with a fixed dictionary size has been widely used in natural language processing (Mikolov et al., 2013; Pennington et al., 2014; Peters et al., 2018; Devlin et al., 2019) due to the discrete nature of tokens, and can be seen recently in contrastive learning (Wu et al., 2018; He et al., 2020) for vision tasks. Instance embedding enables the function to take any possible value on all observed instances but cannot generalize to any unseen token or image.

## 4 RELATED WORK

In this section, we review related problem settings and methods.

**Class-conditional noise (CCN).** Compared with the IDN model, the instance-independent and _class-conditional noise_ (CCN) model has an additional assumption: _p_ ( _Y_[�] _|Y, X_ ) = _p_ ( _Y_[�] _|Y_ ), i.e., the noisy label _Y_[�] only depends on the true label _Y_ . CCN has been well studied in both binary (Angluin & Laird, 1988; Long & Servedio, 2010; Natarajan et al., 2013; Van Rooyen et al., 2015; Liu & Tao, 2015) and multiclass (Patrini et al., 2017; Xia et al., 2019; Yao et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2021b; Zhu et al., 2021) classification. Also, _robust loss functions_ (Ghosh et al., 2017; Zhang & Sabuncu, 2018; Wang et al., 2019b; Charoenphakdee et al., 2019; Ma et al., 2020; Feng et al., 2020; Lyu & Tsang, 2020; Liu & Guo, 2020) have been mainly developed under the CCN setting. In practice, CCN methods can serve as practical approximations of IDN but the assumption could be too strong to fit some real-world data well (Xiao et al., 2015).

**Label smoothing.** Note that Eq. (8) is similar to the _label smoothing_ (LS) technique (Szegedy et al., 2016; Pereyra et al., 2017; Lukasik et al., 2020), where the empirical distribution is linearly interpolated with a uniform distribution with a fixed mixing parameter. It is also related to the soft/hard _bootstrapping loss_ (Reed et al., 2015), where the observed label is mixed with the predicted probability/predicted label. In contrast, in our method, it is the prediction _**p**_ that is “smoothed”, not the label. We elucidate their relations and differences in Appendix C.

**Temperature scaling.** If we use softmax as the final layer of the neural network for _pφ_ ( _Y |X_ ), the proposed method is closely related to the _temperature scaling_ (TS) technique (Guo et al., 2017). Concretely, if _**p** i ∝_ exp _{fi_ ( _X_ ; _φ_ ) _}_ for _i_ = 1 _, . . . , K_ , then Eq. (9) becomes

which shows that _C_ is the _reciprocal of the temperature_ . The difference is that the parameter _C_ is instance-dependent in our formulation, rather than being fixed for all instances. Also, TS (Guo et al., 2017) and its extensions (Kull et al., 2019; Rahimi et al., 2020) have been mainly used as post-hoc confidence calibration methods, while our method is used during training.

**Sample selection.** In a broader sense, the proposed method belongs to a category of methods that treat training examples differently in order to reduce the harmful effects of mislabeled instances. Besides the class-posteriors that our method uses, these methods exploit the training dynamics, loss characteristics, gradient information, or information of data itself from various perspectives. Examples include _data cleansing_ (Liu et al., 2008; Northcutt et al., 2019; Hara et al., 2019) that first removes harmful instances and then (re-)trains the model on the remaining subset; _dynamic training sample selection_ (Malach & Shalev-Shwartz, 2017; Jiang et al., 2018; Han et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2018; Yu et al., 2019; Mirzasoleiman et al., 2020; Wu et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2021; Cheng et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2021a) that selects training examples dynamically during training; _training techniques_ (Menon et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2020) that are designed to increase robustness and avoid memorization of noisy labels; _learning with rejection_ or _selective classification_ (El-Yaniv & Wiener, 2010; Thulasidasan et al., 2019; Mozannar & Sontag, 2020; Charoenphakdee et al., 2021) that abstains from using confusing instances while improving the classification performance on accepted instances; and _semi-supervised learning_ (Nguyen et al., 2020; Li et al., 2020) that exploits unlabeled data. We discuss the relationship with some of them in more detail in Appendix D.

In the same spirit, our proposed method also attempts to detect harmful instances and reduce their influences automatically so as to improve the robustness of the class-posterior estimation. However, unlike explicit sample selection methods, the resulting algorithm is lightweight and has a low computational cost. Also, because the proposed method only affects the class-posterior, it is usually compatible with other training methods. Thus, the proposed method can be used alone or integrated into an existing training pipeline to further improve the performance.

## 5 EXPERIMENTS

In this section, we experimentally verify if the proposed _instance-confidence embedding_ (ICE) method is able to differentiate mislabeled instances from correct ones and consequently improve the classification performance. We also demonstrate that there already exist ambiguous or mislabeled training examples in the original datasets which can be detected by the proposed method.

## 5.1 IMAGE CLASSIFICATION

**Datasets.** We evaluated our method on six image classification datasets, namely **MNIST** (LeCun et al., 1998), Fashion-MNIST ( **FMNIST** ) (Xiao et al., 2017), Kuzushiji-MNIST ( **KMNIST** ) (Clanuwat et al., 2018), **SVHN** (Netzer et al., 2011), **CIFAR-10** , and **CIFAR-100** (Krizhevsky, 2009) datasets. We used a method similar to the one used in Zhang et al. (2021a) to generate instancedependent label noises whose overall noise rate is around 52%. See Appendix E for details and more experimental results.

**Methods.** We compared the following ten methods: (1) ( **CCE** ) categorical cross-entropy loss; (2) ( **Bootstrapping** ) (hard) bootstrapping loss (Reed et al., 2015) that regularizes the output with the predicted label; (3) ( **Adaptation** ) noise adaptation layer (Goldberger & Ben-Reuven, 2017) that estimates a full _K × K_ transition matrix for each instance; (4) ( **Forward** ) forward correction (Patrini et al., 2017) that estimates a transition matrix for all instances; (5) ( **Dual-T** ) dual-T estimator (Yao et al., 2020) that uses the normalized confusion matrix to correct the transition matrix; (6) ( **DAC** ) deep abstaining classifier (Thulasidasan et al., 2019) that uses abstention for robust learning; (7) ( **GCE** ) generalized cross-entropy loss (Zhang & Sabuncu, 2018) as a robust loss; (8) ( **PLC** ) progressive label correction (Zhang et al., 2021a); (9) ( **ICE-LIN** ) instance-confidence embedding with the linear interpolation (Eq. (8)); and (10) ( **ICE-POW** ) the one with the power transformation (Eq. (9)). For a fair comparison, we implemented aforementioned methods using the same network architecture and hyperparameters.

**Models.** For MNIST, FMNIST, and KMNIST, we used a sequential convolutional neural network (CNN) and an Adam optimizer (Kingma & Ba, 2015). For SVHN, CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100, we used a residual network model ResNet-18 (He et al., 2016) and a stochastic gradient descent (SGD) optimizer with momentum (Sutskever et al., 2013). Hyperparameters are provided in Appendix E.

**Improving classification performance.** To verify if the proposed method is able to improve the classification performance under label noise, we constructed semi-synthetic noisy datasets so that the true labels are known. We regarded the original labels as clean labels, although as will be shown in the next experiment, label errors already exist in the original datasets to some extent. We ran 10 trials and reported the means and standard deviations of the test accuracy in Table 1.

We can observe that the proposed method generally outperforms the baseline methods. It is worth noting that when there are a large number of classes (e.g., CIFAR-100), estimating a full transition matrix for each instance (Adaptation) may deteriorate drastically because it requires an additional _K × K_ output and the estimation error could be high. On the contrary, the complexity of our single-parameter approximation does not increase as the number of classes increases. Additionally, in the ridgeline plots in Fig. 4, we can observe the separation of instances with flipped/original labels using the learned confidence _C_ , which may explain the performance improvement.

**Detecting ambiguous/mislabeled instances.** Next, we demonstrate that the proposed method can be used for detecting ambiguous or possibly mislabeled instances. We trained the model on the original datasets with the proposed method. A benefit of using a single-parameter approximation is that it naturally derives an order of the training examples. We sorted the training examples via the confidence and showed the 32 most low-confidence ones in Fig. 5. We also extracted high/low-confidence training examples for each classes as shown in Fig. 6. Results of other datasets are provided in Appendix E.

We can observe that, surprisingly, in these supposedly clean datasets, a number of instance might be mislabeled. In MNIST and SVHN, we found clearly mislabeled images. There are ambiguous images such as 2-7 and 4-9 pairs in MNIST and shirt/T-shirt/pullover/coat photos in Fashion-MNIST. In CIFAR-10, it is interesting that images in the animal category are more likely to have a low confidence. We conjecture that the _spurious correlation_ between the object and the background color plays an important role. We also found multi-modality issues, e.g., kiwi, owl, and chicken are all labeled as bird but are not _visually prototypical_ birds. This phenomenon suggests the possibility of using the proposed method for diagnosing label issues in large-scale datasets.

## 5.2 TEXT CLASSIFICATION

We discovered that noisy label issues also exist in text datasets. We conducted similar experiments on the GLUE benchmark (Wang et al., 2019a), which is a collection of datasets for natural language understanding. We trained a BERT-base model pretrained using a masked language modeling (MLM) objective (Devlin et al., 2019) with a default AdamW optimizer (Loshchilov & Hutter, 2017). The performance in terms of the suggested evaluation metric was reported in Table 2.

We can observe that, except on the RTE dataset, the performance was improved or approximately the same compared with the default CCE method, which shows the benefits of using instance-specifically adjusted confidences. Although, if the dataset is relatively clean, the improvement might be marginal.

We also found mislabeled or ambiguous instances in these datasets. A typical example is the Corpus of Linguistic Acceptability (CoLA) (Warstadt et al., 2019) dataset, which consists of English grammatical acceptability judgments. Six selected low-confidence training examples are given in Table 3. We found that several ungrammatical sentences were mislabeled as acceptable, and some _syntactically acceptable_ sentences were labeled as unacceptable by annotators possibly because they have _semantic errors_ . In this way, we may use the proposed method to probe if the model prediction is consistent with our intent. More results are provided in Appendix E.

## 6 CONCLUSION

We have introduced a novel variational approximation of the instance-dependent noise (IDN) model, referred to as _instance-confidence embedding_ (ICE). Compared with existing methods based on the class-conditional noise (CCN) assumption, the proposed method is able to capture instance-specific noise information and consequently improve the classification performance. The use of the onedimensional instance embedding naturally derives an order of training examples which can be used for detecting ambiguous or mislabeled instances. For future directions, it is interesting to explore its combination with other training techniques and its extensions in data cleansing, learning with rejection, or active learning.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: This paper presents a compelling and well-motivated approach to the complex problem of instance-dependent noise (IDN) in multiclass classification. By proposing the "instance-confidence embedding" (ICE) method, the authors offer a pragmatic, domain-agnostic alternative to the computationally expensive and parameter-heavy methods typically required to estimate instance-specific noise transition matrices. The introduction is particularly strong, establishing a clear narrative arc that moves from the limitations of the standard class-conditional noise (CCN) assumption to the specific utility of identifying ambiguous instances through latent leaf-node embeddings. However, the most significant risk for the paper lies in its mathematical justification; while the proposed scalar confidence parameter elegantly simplifies the model, the authors must ensure they rigorously address the validity of this approximation—specifically, whether collapsing the $K \times K$ transition matrix into a single-scalar confidence variable sacrifices too much nuance in identifying specific inter-class confusion patterns compared to more expressive IDN characterizations. Provided the experimental section demonstrates that this simplification acts as an effective regularizer rather than an over-simplification, the work appears to be a high-quality contribution to robust learning.

progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: This paper addresses an important and challenging problem—instance-dependent label noise—by proposing a variational approximation that reduces the complexity of estimating per-instance transition matrices to a single scalar confidence parameter, combined with instance embeddings to capture non-smooth mappings. The motivation is clearly articulated, the related work is appropriately situated, and the graphical model provides a concise overview of the proposed approach. However, the excerpt reveals several significant concerns. First, the reliance on per-instance trainable parameters raises substantial risks of overfitting and poor generalization, especially for large-scale datasets, and the paper does not yet discuss regularization strategies or scaling behavior. Second, the variational approximation that collapses the \(K \times K\) transition matrix to a single scalar is a strong simplification whose theoretical justification and error bounds are not provided; it remains unclear how well this captures the true instance-dependent noise structure or under what conditions it might fail. Third, the claim that the mapping “is usually not required to generalize to unseen examples” is at odds with the typical goal of learning systems, limiting the method’s practical applicability unless the task is solely data cleansing or debugging. Finally, the lack of any experimental results or empirical validation in the provided text makes it impossible to assess the method’s effectiveness or compare it to baselines. Overall, while the idea is novel and well-motivated, the paper would benefit from a deeper theoretical analysis of the approximation, a clear discussion of overfitting and scalability, and a demonstration of competitive performance on realistic benchmarks.

(3) later) and the fact $\mathbf{p}$ is a probability vector make this clear to a reader familiar with the field. This is a minor expositional gap, not a mathematical error.

comments.title: Incomplete justification for 'single-scalar' approximation adequacy
comments.quote: Noting the fact that most columns of the IDN transition matrix have only limited influence on the class-posterior estimation, we propose a variational approximation that uses a single-scalar _confidence_ parameter.
comments.explanation: The claim that 'most columns have limited influence' relies on the clean posterior being nearly deterministic (one component near 1). The paper acknowledges this implicitly ('If the maximum value of $p_\phi(Y|x)$ is close to 1...'). However, this assumption may not hold for instances that are inherently ambiguous (e.g., near decision boundaries), which are precisely the cases where instance-dependent noise matters most. The authors later use $C$ to model such instances (low confidence). The argument for using a single scalar is heuristically motivated, not rigorously justified (e.g., no bound on approximation error). This is a moderate gap: the paper does not quantify when this approximation is safe.

comments.title: Underspecified transformation functions for confidence
comments.quote: $h(\mathbf{p}; C)_i = C p_i + \frac{1-C}{K}$   (linear interpolation) and $h(\mathbf{p}; C)_i = \frac{p_i^C}{\sum_j p_j^C}$   (power transformation).
comments.explanation: Both transformations are presented without justification for why these specific forms were chosen, nor is a criterion given for selecting between them. The paper states they are both 'argmax-preserving' and satisfy boundary conditions at $C=0$ and $C=1$, but does not discuss whether they are equally expressive or whether one is more appropriate for certain noise structures. The code appendix might clarify, but at this point, a reader cannot reproduce the method without arbitrary choice. This is a moderate underspecification.

comments.title: Linear combination of columns vs. rows in transition matrix
comments.quote: Note that _pφ_ ( _Y_[] _|x_ ) can be seen as a linear combination of columns of _**T**_ ( _x_ ) weighted by _pφ_ ( _Y |x_ ); If the maximum value of _pφ_ ( _Y |x_ ) is close to 1, i.e., the label of the instance _x_ is almost deterministic, the estimation of _K −_ 1 columns of _**T**_ ( _x_ ) has only limited influence on the estimated noisy class-posterior _p_ ( _Y_[] _|x_ ).
comments.explanation: The transition matrix T(x) is defined as T_{ij}(x) = p(Ỹ=j|Y=i, X=x), so rows correspond to the true label. The noisy posterior is p(Ỹ|x) = Σ_i p(Y=i|x) T_{i·}(x), which is a linear combination of rows of T, not columns. The subsequent argument about limited influence when p(Y|x) is nearly deterministic refers to the fact that only the row for the true class matters; the other K−1 rows (not columns) have negligible weight. The text says 'columns' in both sentences, which contradicts the definition and could mislead readers about the algebraic structure. The intended meaning is clear from context (the argument works for rows), but the notation is incorrect.

comments.title: Potentially flawed variational bound derivation
comments.quote: Then, let us consider the _expected log-likelihood_ as the learning objective, which can be rewritten as where _D_ KL denotes the Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence, and the second term is Since the KL-divergence is always non-negative, this term gives a _variational lower bound_ of the expected log-likelihood. Then, we have the following learning objective to maximize:
comments.explanation: The text describes rewriting the expected log-likelihood as E[log q] plus a KL divergence, then claiming E[log q] is a lower bound. This decomposition is standard when the expectation is taken under the model's own distribution (i.e., E_{p_φ}[log p_φ] = E_{p_φ}[log q] + D_KL(p_φ||q)). However, the learning objective in supervised learning is typically the expected log-likelihood under the data-generating distribution p_data, not under the model. comments.title: Unclear justification for the argmax-preserving requirement

progressive_original__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive Original (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive_original__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: This paper addresses an important and challenging problem—instance-dependent label noise—by proposing a variational approximation that reduces the complexity of estimating per-instance transition matrices to a single scalar confidence parameter, combined with instance embeddings to capture non-smooth mappings. The motivation is clearly articulated, the related work is appropriately situated, and the graphical model provides a concise overview of the proposed approach. However, the excerpt reveals several significant concerns. First, the reliance on per-instance trainable parameters raises substantial risks of overfitting and poor generalization, especially for large-scale datasets, and the paper does not yet discuss regularization strategies or scaling behavior. Second, the variational approximation that collapses the \(K \times K\) transition matrix to a single scalar is a strong simplification whose theoretical justification and error bounds are not provided; it remains unclear how well this captures the true instance-dependent noise structure or under what conditions it might fail. Third, the claim that the mapping “is usually not required to generalize to unseen examples” is at odds with the typical goal of learning systems, limiting the method’s practical applicability unless the task is solely data cleansing or debugging. Finally, the lack of any experimental results or empirical validation in the provided text makes it impossible to assess the method’s effectiveness or compare it to baselines. Overall, while the idea is novel and well-motivated, the paper would benefit from a deeper theoretical analysis of the approximation, a clear discussion of overfitting and scalability, and a demonstration of competitive performance on realistic benchmarks.

progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: This paper addresses the relevant and challenging problem of instance-dependent noise (IDN), offering a clear motivation to extend beyond the restrictive class-conditional assumption, and the problem formulation is well-defined with an effective graphical illustration. However, the manuscript raises significant concerns regarding the method's validity, robustness, and scalability. First, the use of per-instance trainable embeddings, explicitly acknowledged as not requiring generalization to unseen examples, risks reducing the model to a noise lookup table that memorizes training samples rather than learning a principled noise process; this undermines the fundamental goal of noise-robust generalization and requires a rigorous argument that such memorization does not lead to overfitting or failure to transfer learned denoising signals to the test set. Second, the approximation of the full $K \times K$ transition matrix with a single scalar confidence parameter is a drastic simplification that appears insufficiently justified; the claim that most matrix columns have limited influence is speculative, and the mechanism for recovering transition probabilities from the scalar must be clearly detailed and validated to ensure it captures essential noise structure without introducing bias. Finally, the scalability of maintaining a unique trainable parameter for every instance in large-scale datasets warrants discussion. The authors must address how ICE mitigates memorization risks, substantiate the fidelity of the scalar approximation, and clarify the implications for generalization to align with the technical standards expected at ICLR.

comments.title: Misattribution of Goldberger & Ben-Reuven (2017) for instance-dependent noise estimation
comments.quote: A straightforward method is to jointly estimate the matrix-valued function _**T**_ ( _x_ ) as well as the clean class-posterior _pφ_ ( _Y |X_ ) using neural networks (Goldberger & Ben-Reuven, 2017).
comments.explanation: The paper cites Goldberger & Ben-Reuven (2017) as an example of estimating an instance-dependent transition matrix **T**(x). However, the cited work explicitly assumes *class-conditional noise* (CCN) and estimates a static transition matrix T_{ij} = P(Y~=j|Y=i) that is constant across all instances X. It does not model or estimate an instance-dependent function T(x). This mischaracterization misaligns the paper's literature positioning, though it does not undermine the proposed ICE method itself.

comments.title: Contradiction in training setup description
comments.quote:
For MNIST, FMNIST, and KMNIST, we used a sequential convolutional neural network (CNN) and an Adam optimizer (Kingma & Ba, 2015). For SVHN, CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100, we used a residual network model ResNet-18 (He et al., 2016) and a stochastic gradient descent (SGD) optimizer with momentum (Sutskever et al., 2013).

For a fair comparison, we implemented aforementioned methods using the same network architecture and hyperparameters.
comments.explanation: The authors correctly tailor architectures and optimizers to different datasets, which is standard practice. However, they immediately claim that all compared methods were implemented using 'the same network architecture and hyperparameters'. This is a direct contradiction. The intent is clearly that hyperparameters were held constant *within each dataset* across all baselines and ICE variants, but the phrasing is ambiguous and misleading. Clarifying that the constraint applies per-dataset would resolve this.

comments.title: Highly suspicious overall noise rate
comments.quote: We used a method similar to the one used in Zhang et al. (2021a) to generate instancedependent label noises whose overall noise rate is around 52%.
comments.explanation: An overall noise rate of ~52% is extremely high for standard image classification benchmarks, approaching random guessing and explaining the notably low baseline accuracies. This specific figure is unusual and likely a typographical error (e.g., intending 20%, 30%, or 40%). If accurate, it represents a near-random noise regime that severely limits practical relevance and requires explicit justification. The authors should verify this value and clarify whether it was chosen deliberately for an extreme-case analysis.

comments.title: Overstated justification for argmax-preserving constraint
comments.quote: This is because _h_ should only affect the confidence of the prediction, not the final decision. Otherwise, if _h_ is too flexible and is able to map a confident prediction to a different confident prediction, then the output of _f_ could be arbitrary, and consequently, no information of the true label can be learned from the noisy label supervision.
comments.explanation: The authors impose an argmax-preserving constraint on $h$ to ensure $C$ modulates certainty rather than flipping predictions. However, the justification that violating this would make $f$'s output 'arbitrary' and prevent learning is logically overstated. In noisy label learning, models often benefit from being able to correct mislabeled instances. The constraint is likely imposed for model identifiability or to prevent $C$ from absorbing label-correction information, but the stated reasoning conflates flexibility with a complete breakdown of learning. A clearer explanation regarding identifiability or the semantic role of $C$ would strengthen this section.

comments.title: Questionable statistical testing methodology in results table
comments.quote: Outperforming methods are highlighted in boldface using one-tailed t-tests with a significance level of 0 _._ 05.
comments.explanation: The table caption specifies one-tailed t-tests for multiple pairwise comparisons across different datasets and methods. Unless the authors had a strict, pre-registered directional hypothesis for each comparison, one-tailed tests are statistically questionable in benchmarking tables as they inflate the Type I error rate. Standard ML evaluation practice typically defaults to two-tailed tests or non-parametric alternatives. This could lead readers to overstate the statistical significance of the reported improvements.

comments.title: Ambiguous reference to 'complexity' scaling
comments.quote: On the contrary, the complexity of our single-parameter approximation does not increase as the number of classes increases.
comments.explanation: The claim that complexity does not increase with $K$ raises ambiguity between parameter count and computational (time/space) complexity. The authors clearly intend to highlight parameter efficiency ($1$ vs $O(K^2)$), but the computational cost of evaluating the transformation $h(\mathbf{p}; C)$ scales linearly with $K$. The unqualified term 'complexity' could mislead readers evaluating runtime overhead. Specifying 'parameter complexity' would resolve this.

comments.title: Metric unit inconsistency in Table 2 caption
comments.quote: Table 2: **Performance** (%) on the GLUE benchmark for natural language understanding.
comments.explanation: The caption denotes all reported metrics as percentages (%). However, the accompanying text explicitly states that the Matthews correlation coefficient (Mcc) is used for the CoLA task. Mcc is a correlation metric typically reported on a [-1, 1] or [0, 1] scale, not inherently a percentage. While it is standard practice to scale these values by 100 for table presentation, stating "(%)" in the caption without clarification is slightly inconsistent with the explicit metric definition.

comments.title: Notation and conceptual ambiguities in transition matrix formulation
comments.quote: Note that _pφ_ ( _Y_[] _|x_ ) can be seen as a linear combination of columns of _**T**_ ( _x_ ) weighted by _pφ_ ( _Y |x_ )
comments.explanation: This excerpt contains two related ambiguities. First, a transposition convention mismatch: the definition _**T**_{ij}(x) := p(\tilde{Y}=j|Y=i, X=x) implies that marginalizing over Y corresponds to a linear combination of the _rows_ of _**T**(x), not the columns. Second, a notation conflation: the subscript _\phi_ is used for both the clean class-posterior and the noisy class-posterior. Technically, the noisy posterior depends on both the clean posterior and the unknown noise matrix _**T**(x), so sharing the subscript blurs the distinction between the model's learned estimate and the true distribution. While common in ML literature, clarifying the row/column convention and distinguishing the subscripts would improve mathematical precision.

progressive_original__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive Original (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive_original__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: This paper addresses the relevant and challenging problem of instance-dependent noise (IDN), offering a clear motivation to extend beyond the restrictive class-conditional assumption, and the problem formulation is well-defined with an effective graphical illustration. However, the manuscript raises significant concerns regarding the method's validity, robustness, and scalability. First, the use of per-instance trainable embeddings, explicitly acknowledged as not requiring generalization to unseen examples, risks reducing the model to a noise lookup table that memorizes training samples rather than learning a principled noise process; this undermines the fundamental goal of noise-robust generalization and requires a rigorous argument that such memorization does not lead to overfitting or failure to transfer learned denoising signals to the test set. Second, the approximation of the full $K \times K$ transition matrix with a single scalar confidence parameter is a drastic simplification that appears insufficiently justified; the claim that most matrix columns have limited influence is speculative, and the mechanism for recovering transition probabilities from the scalar must be clearly detailed and validated to ensure it captures essential noise structure without introducing bias. Finally, the scalability of maintaining a unique trainable parameter for every instance in large-scale datasets warrants discussion. The authors must address how ICE mitigates memorization risks, substantiate the fidelity of the scalar approximation, and clarify the implications for generalization to align with the technical standards expected at ICLR.

comments.title: Misattribution of Goldberger & Ben-Reuven (2017) for instance-dependent noise estimation
comments.quote: A straightforward method is to jointly estimate the matrix-valued function _**T**_ ( _x_ ) as well as the clean class-posterior _pφ_ ( _Y |X_ ) using neural networks (Goldberger & Ben-Reuven, 2017).
comments.explanation: Initially, I noted the citation of Goldberger & Ben-Reuven (2017) as a direct example of estimating an instance-dependent transition matrix **T**(x). Upon verifying the cited work, I realized it explicitly assumes *class-conditional noise* (CCN) and estimates a static transition matrix T_{ij} = P(Y~=j|Y=i) that is constant across all instances X. It does not model or estimate an instance-dependent function T(x). The surrounding context does not resolve this, as the sentence explicitly links the citation to estimating T(x). This mischaracterization slightly misaligns the paper's literature positioning, though it does not undermine the proposed ICE method itself.

comments.title: Overcounting parameters for a stochastic transition matrix
comments.quote: Without any restriction, we need _K × K_ parameters for each instance _x_ .
comments.explanation: The text claims that approximating the IDN transition matrix requires K × K parameters per instance. While the matrix is indeed of size K × K, a valid noise transition matrix must be row-stochastic (each row sums to 1). This imposes K linear constraints, reducing the number of independent parameters to K(K - 1). I initially thought this might be a critical claim about model complexity, but it appears the authors used K × K as loose shorthand for the matrix dimensions rather than counting free degrees of freedom. While common in ML writing, it is technically imprecise in a probability/constraint context and could slightly mislead readers about the actual parameter burden being mitigated.

comments.title: Missing mathematical expression in Section 2.1 due to OCR extraction
comments.quote:
We assume that the _clean class-posterior p_ ( _Y |X_ ) comes from a parametric family of distributions:

where _**p** ∈_ ∆ _[K][−]_[1] is the probability parameter for _Y_ in the ( _K −_ 1)-dimensional probability simplex ∆ _[K][−]_[1] , and _f_ : _X →_ ∆ _[K][−]_[1] is a differentiable function parameterized by _φ_ that maps the feature _X_ to its corresponding probability parameter _**p**_ .
comments.explanation: The transition from 'distributions:' to 'where **p**...' is abrupt and lacks a defining equation. I suspected a missing definition or formatting error. Given the context, the omitted equation is almost certainly the standard link function (e.g., `p(Y|X) = softmax(f(X))` or `p(Y=k|X) = exp(f_k(X)) / Σ_j exp(f_j(X))`). This is a clear artifact of PDF-to-text OCR failing to capture a centered display math block. The prose immediately clarifies the role of **p** and `f(X)`, so the mathematical intent is recoverable, but the missing formula creates a continuity gap that a careful reader would need to infer.

comments.title: Transposition convention mismatch in transition matrix linear combination
comments.quote: Note that _pφ_ ( _Y_[] _|x_ ) can be seen as a linear combination of columns of _**T**_ ( _x_ ) weighted by _pφ_ ( _Y |x_ )
comments.explanation: The authors define the noise transition matrix as _**T**_{ij}(x) := p(\tilde{Y}=j|Y=i, X=x), meaning the i-th row corresponds to a fixed true class Y=i. Consequently, marginalizing over Y to compute the noisy posterior corresponds to a linear combination of the _rows_ of _**T**(x), not the columns. The text's reference to 'columns' contradicts their own index definition (unless they implicitly assume a transposed matrix-vector convention _**q** =_ **T**_**p**, which would require _**T**_{ji} to store the conditional probabilities). This is a minor notation inconsistency that might confuse a careful reader checking the linear algebra, though the underlying intuition about dimensionality reduction remains sound.

comments.title: Notation conflation of clean and noisy class-posteriors
comments.quote: Note that _pφ_ ( _Y_[] _|x_ ) can be seen as a linear combination of columns of _**T**_ ( _x_ ) weighted by _pφ_ ( _Y |x_ )
comments.explanation: The parameter _\phi_ is explicitly introduced to define the clean class-posterior _f_\phi :_ X →_ **p** (i.e., _p_\phi(Y|X)). However, the same subscript _\phi_ is used here to denote the noisy class-posterior _p_\phi(\tilde{Y}|x). Technically, the noisy posterior depends on both the clean posterior and the unknown noise matrix _**T**(x), so it should not carry the _\phi_ subscript unless referring to a specific model approximation. While the authors correctly introduce a variational approximation _q_\theta,\phi_ in Section 3.1 to formalize this, the earlier phrasing loosely conflates the model's estimate of the noisy posterior with the true distribution. This is a common but technically imprecise shorthand in ML literature.

comments.title: Ambiguity linking model prediction confidence to true label determinism
comments.quote: If the maximum value of _pφ_ ( _Y |x_ ) is close to 1, i.e., the label of the instance _x_ is almost deterministic,
comments.explanation: The sentence equates a high maximum probability from the model's prediction _p_\phi(Y|x) with the true label being 'almost deterministic'. However, _p_\phi is merely a learned approximation; its peak value does not guarantee the true posterior is peaked (e.g., due to model bias, class imbalance, or early training stages). The justification for the single-parameter approximation fundamentally relies on the _true_ underlying posterior being near-deterministic, which dictates that only the dominant column/row of _**T**(x) matters. The text's phrasing blurs the distinction between the model's current confidence and the data-generating process. This is a minor framing ambiguity, but the intended probabilistic reasoning is clear from context.

progressive__glm-4.7-flash.label: Progressive (glm-4.7-flash)
progressive__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: This paper addresses the limitation of standard class-conditional noise assumptions by investigating learning under instance-dependent noise (IDN). The proposed Instance-Confidence Embedding (ICE) method offers a sensible variational approximation by simplifying the computationally expensive instance-specific noise transition matrices into a single scalar confidence parameter. However, there is a potential concern regarding the proposed "instance embedding" mechanism, which the authors state assigns a trainable parameter to each training instance. For large-scale datasets (e.g., ImageNet with over a million samples), adding a trainable parameter per instance constitutes a massive over-parameterization ($O(N)$) regarding the noise structure, effectively allowing the model to memorize the training set's noise rather than learning a generalized corruption process. Consequently, the paper must rigorously justify this parameterization against more standard regularization techniques, specifically demonstrating that the instance-specific parameters do not simply serve as a noise mask for overfitting rather than a robust model of the instance-dependent noise distribution.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of Label Smoothing Inputs
comments.quote: Note that Eq. (8) is similar to the _label smoothing_ (LS) technique (Szegedy et al., 2016; Pereyra et al., 2017; Lukasik et al., 2020), where the empirical distribution is linearly interpolated with a uniform distribution with a fixed mixing parameter.
comments.explanation: The text describes label smoothing (LS) as using an 'empirical distribution' as the input to the interpolation. Strictly speaking, LS interpolates the *one-hot empirical label* (the ground truth) with a uniform prior. In the authors' method (Eq 8), the interpolation takes the *predicted posterior* $p$, not the label. While the authors immediately correct this by stating 'In contrast, in our method, it is the prediction p that is 'smoothed'', the initial setup of the comparison is technically imprecise and could mislead a reader regarding the fundamental difference between LS and the proposed method (which involves noise modeling rather than smoothing the training target).

comments.title: Ambiguous Parameter Count Comparison
comments.quote: The number of instances is usually not as large as the number of parameters of the classifier f (e.g., CIFAR-10 (Krizhevsky, 2009): 5 _×_ 10[4] , ResNet-18 (He et al., 2016): _∼_ 1 _×_ 10[7] )
comments.explanation: The text contrasts the 'number of instances' (N) with the 'number of parameters' (P). The example for CIFAR-10 (50,000) is clearly the number of training samples, not model parameters. The formatting places these values in close proximity, and for CIFAR-10, the value 50k does not represent a model parameter count (standard models have significantly more). A reader could incorrectly infer that a CIFAR-10 model has only 50k parameters based on the listing style, which would contradict the author's intent to argue that models are over-parameterized compared to datasets.

comments.title: Weak Analogy to Contrastive Learning
comments.quote: The idea of associating an entity with a scalar or vector embedding using a simple lookup table with a fixed dictionary size has been widely used in natural language processing ... and can be seen recently in contrastive learning (Wu et al., 2018; He et al., 2020) for vision tasks.
comments.explanation: The authors cite 'contrastive learning' as a precedent for instance embedding (lookup tables). However, modern contrastive learning methods (like SimCLR, MoCo) are designed to learn *generalizable* representations for *all* data using shared parameters, not to learn non-generalizable, per-instance parameters for each training example. The authors correctly note that instance embedding 'cannot generalize to any unseen token or image', but the analogy to contrastive learning—which specifically aims to avoid memorization—is conceptually weak and could confuse a reader about the methodology's generalization properties.

comments.title: Unreadable Experimental Data Due to OCR Artifacts
comments.explanation: The table presents numerical results (Accuracy and Standard Deviation) for the ICE-LIN and ICE-POW methods. The OCR extraction has stripped out decimal points, rendering the data unintelligible (e.g., '9739' should likely be '97.39', '025' should likely be '0.25'). Assuming the most plausible intended symbol (adding decimal points where digits are grouped), the values (MNIST: 97.39, FMNIST: 87.07, KMNIST: 87.08, SVHN: 77.16, CIFAR-10: 82.07, CIFAR-100: 56.04) appear to be consistent with the datasets' difficulty and the proposed method's performance. However, the standard deviation for SVHN is noted as '219' in the raw text, which is unusually high (21.9%) for an accuracy metric reported with a mean; however, given the OCR error, this could also be a misread of a lower value (e.g., 0.52). Regardless of the specific value, the text provided presents the data in a broken format that would confuse any reader attempting to verify the results.

comments.explanation: The Figure 5 caption contains text extracted from the PDF image. In this example, the OCR has mashed the words 'label' and the label 'deer' together with 'guess', resulting in 'label: deerguess'. comments.title: Phrasing confusion regarding 'clean labels' in semi-synthetic setup
comments.quote: We regarded the original labels as clean labels, although as will be shown in the next experiment, label errors already exist in the original datasets to some extent.
comments.explanation: The text initially asserts that the original labels were treated as 'clean' to evaluate the method. However, the subsequent parenthetical immediately admits that these labels contain errors. This creates a logical contradiction regarding the experimental setup: if the evaluation targets 'clean' labels but the dataset actually has 52% noise (as noted in the paper's assumptions), the reported test accuracy may be unreliable or misinterpreted. The sentence conflates the 'original' source data (which may have intrinsic noise) with the 'semi-synthetic' construction (where noise is applied). It should be clarified whether the evaluation metric refers to the *true* latent labels (ground truth) or the flawed original labels.

comments.title: Misuse of 'multi-modality' terminology
comments.quote: We also found multi-modality issues, e.g., kiwi, owl, and chicken are all labeled as bird but are not visually prototypical birds.
comments.explanation: In the context of the 'animal category' and 'visually prototypical', the authors are describing visual ambiguity or class-conditional heterogeneity rather than 'multi-modality'. In computer vision, 'multi-modality' typically refers to inputs involving multiple modalities (e.g., vision and audio) or feature distributions with multiple peaks. Describing these instances as having 'multi-modality issues' is technically incorrect and confusing for a reader familiar with the standard definition of the term.

comments.title: Potential misuse of 'ridgeline plots'
comments.quote: In the ridgeline plots in Fig. 4, we can observe the separation of instances with flipped/original labels using the learned confidence C
comments.explanation: The term 'ridgeline plot' (or joyplot) is used to describe plots of stacked density curves. The authors mention observing 'separation of instances' using a scalar confidence value. It is unclear if the plot visualizes distributions (density) of confidence scores or simply ranks instances (e.g., a vertical line plot or ranked list). Referring to a simple ranking or confidence profile as a 'ridgeline plot' may be a misuse of the visualization terminology, potentially misleading readers about the visual content of Figure 4.
